troubleshooting.title: Debug an XPath expression that returns unexpected results
troubleshooting.shortdesc: XPath queries that seem correct but return unexpected results are usually caused by the flat HTML structure that markdown-validator produces. Use the REPL to inspect the actual HTML and adjust your query.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Debug unexpected XPath query results in markdown-validator, including flat DOM limitations, namespace issues, and following-sibling behavior.

condition.title: Situation
condition.p: An XPath expression in a body rule returns no results, or it returns more elements than expected, causing the rule to behave incorrectly.

cause.title: Cause: Flat HTML — within-section containment does not work
cause.p: The rendered HTML is flat: all headings are direct children of <body>. Queries like .//h2[1]/following-sibling::p do not return only paragraphs in the first H2's section — they return all paragraphs after the first H2, including those in every subsequent section.

step.cmd: In the REPL, run dump html on your document to see the actual HTML structure.

info.codeblock: md-validate repl > load docs/tutorial.md > dump html
info.p: Confirm that headings are direct children of <body> with no section wrappers.

step.cmd: Rewrite the XPath to use absolute position instead of containment.

info.p: Checking for the presence of the heading rather than its content achieves the same editorial goal without requiring containment.

cause.title: Cause: Whitespace in element text
cause.p: XPath text comparisons are exact. A heading with a trailing space (Prerequisites ) does not match the predicate [text()='Prerequisites'].

step.cmd: In the REPL, run the query with text to see the exact string the element contains, including any whitespace.

info.p: Note any trailing spaces in the output.

step.cmd: Update the XPath predicate to use normalize-space() instead of a bare text() comparison.

cause.title: Cause: XPath returns zero results
cause.p: The element path does not match any nodes in the HTML. Common reasons: the element name is wrong, the document uses a heading level you did not expect, or the XPath predicate is too strict.

step.cmd: In the REPL, run a broader query first and narrow it down.

info.codeblock:
> query //h2 text Result: ['Prerequisites', 'Deploy the app', 'Clean up resources']
> query //h2[2] text Result: ['Deploy the app']

step.cmd: Compare the actual element text against your predicate value.
